title: Line-by-Line Scroll
subtitle: {key:Ctrl-E} and {key:Ctrl-Y} — scroll without moving the cursor (until you have to).
summary: {key:Ctrl-E} scrolls the screen down one line; {key:Ctrl-Y} scrolls it up. The cursor stays on its file line until the screen would have to push it off — then it moves with the screen.

blocks.text: When you want to *peek* at what's just off-screen without moving the cursor, {key:Ctrl-E} and {key:Ctrl-Y} are the right tools. They scroll the viewport one line at a time and leave the cursor where it was — until the cursor would scroll off, in which case it slides with the screen.
Counts work: {key:5}{key:Ctrl-E} scrolls down 5 lines. With `'scrolloff'` set, the cursor will follow as needed to stay within the offset zone.

blocks.title: Cursor Sticks Until Forced
blocks.text:
{key:Ctrl-E} just decrements the window's *visible top line*. The cursor's buffer position doesn't change. Result: the screen scrolls; the cursor appears to climb upward in the visible region.

When the cursor's line is about to scroll off the *top* (or fall below the bottom for {key:Ctrl-Y}), Vim moves the cursor to keep it visible. So you can scroll past the cursor's line and the cursor follows along.

With `'scrolloff'` > 0, the cursor moves earlier — the offset acts as a margin. Combined with `'cursorline'`, this is a very tactile way to read code while keeping a position marked.

rows: Scroll screen down (1 line; counts work)
